Hardware · ML · BLE
2024

Sensorized
Gesture Glove

ESP32-S3 KiCad 10 BLE Flex Sensors USB-C AP2112K LDO On-device ML

Overview

A custom-designed wearable PCB and firmware system for recognizing hand gestures from flex sensor data. Five flex sensors on the fingers produce analog resistance signals that vary with bend angle; these signals are read through the ESP32-S3's ADC channels, processed, and classified into distinct gestures using a trained classification algorithm.

The project spans the full hardware and software stack: schematic capture, PCB layout, component selection, firmware, data collection, and signal classification — all designed and built from scratch.

What I Built

  • Custom PCB — a circular board designed in KiCad, housing the ESP32-S3-Mini-1, a USB-C receptacle with proper CC resistors for power negotiation, an AP2112K 3.3 V LDO regulator, decoupling capacitors, dual status LEDs, two pushbuttons (including a BOOT button for flashing), I2C and JTAG headers, and five analog input channels for the flex sensors
  • Flex sensor interface — each flex sensor is wired as a voltage divider with a 10 kΩ pull-up resistor, giving a continuous analog bend-angle signal per finger on a dedicated GPIO/ADC pin
  • Schematic and layout — full KiCad schematic with ERC-clean netlist, routed PCB with antenna keepout, USB differential pair routing, and ground plane on both layers
  • Signal classification — collected training data across gesture classes and implemented a classification algorithm in Python to identify distinct hand poses from the five-dimensional sensor readings
  • Firmware — embedded code on the ESP32-S3 to sample the ADC channels, package sensor data, and handle USB communication
V1

Circular Board — Assembled

Circular hand-mounted PCB, fully populated and tested with flex sensors.

Assembled

Assembled Board

The V1 board is a circular PCB designed to sit flush on the back of the hand. The ESP32-S3-Mini-1 module sits centrally, with the power LED glowing red confirming the 3.3 V rail is live. The USB-C receptacle at the left edge provides power and programming access. Flex sensor cables and a USB cable are visible, connecting the five sensor channels to the board.

V1 assembled board — ESP32-S3-Mini-1 · circular form factor · power LED active · flex sensor + USB-C connections

Schematic & Layout

The V1 schematic captures the full signal chain: USB-C receptacle with 5.1 kΩ CC pull-downs for device-mode power, AP2112K LDO stepping VBUS to 3.3 V, five independent flex-sensor voltage dividers on dedicated ADC channels, power indicator LEDs, and programming/reset buttons. The circular layout places the ESP32 module centrally with its antenna cleared of copper on all layers. ERC and DRC pass clean.

V1 KiCad layout — circular form factor · ESP32-S3-Mini-1 placement · antenna keepout · copper pour · ERC/DRC clean

Signal Processing

Each flex sensor produces a noisy, slowly-varying analog signal. The core challenge is extracting reliable gesture classifications from five channels of correlated, non-stationary data. Iterating on the sensor interface, data collection protocol, and classifier architecture taught a careful discipline around noise sources, feature representation, and generalization.

V2

Rectangular Board — Built & Tested

Rectangular bench-test form factor, fully assembled with all components verified.

Built

Assembled Hardware

Version 2 uses a rectangular form factor to simplify bench testing and firmware development before committing to a final wearable shape. The ESP32-S3-Mini-1 is socketed at center, with the USB-C receptacle providing power and programming. Multiple header connectors expose the five flex-sensor inputs, power rails, and GPIO breakouts for rapid iteration.

V2 assembled PCB — ESP32-S3-Mini-1 module · USB-C receptacle · flex-sensor and GPIO header connectors · AP2112K LDO power path

Schematic & Layout

The V2 schematic retains the full V1 signal chain — USB-C with 5.1 kΩ CC pull-downs, AP2112K LDO, five independent flex-sensor voltage dividers, power LEDs, and programming/reset buttons — and refines the passives and routing for the rectangular form factor. All ERC and DRC checks pass clean.

V2 schematic — ESP32-S3-Mini-1 · AP2112K LDO · USB-C · 5× flex-sensor dividers · LEDs · programming buttons
V2 KiCad layout — rectangular form factor · antenna keepout · component placement · trace routing · ERC/DRC clean

Signal Processing

With hardware assembled, the focus shifts to firmware: reading five ADC channels at 100 Hz, applying a lightweight low-pass filter per channel, then feeding the feature vector into a gesture classifier. BLE HID descriptors map recognized gestures to keycodes, so the glove presents as a wireless keyboard to any host device.
